summary: Hotz argues that intellectual property is fundamentally flawed because digital copying isn't theft—everyone gains rather than someone losing. He observes that despite claims of anti-capitalism, most people remain deeply invested in money. His vision for AI is that it should deliver massive value to society while generating zero profit for anyone, similar to the early internet before monetization ruined it. He wants to build open source software that 'market breaks' every sector, driving out grifters and scammers by eliminating capturable value.
key_points: Piracy isn't theft because the original owner still has the item—it's more like taking a picture, a win-win
The early internet delivered huge value without profit, which is why it was good; monetization ruined it
The best outcome for AI would be delivering massive societal value with zero profit to anyone
Open source can be weaponized to 'market break' sectors and eliminate rent-seeking
True indifference to money, not hatred of capitalism, is the real form of not caring about it
money_quotes: Intellectual property is a really dumb idea.
Because when you smash the windows and grab the stuff, Tiffany's no longer has the stuff. With piracy, everyone has the stuff.
The opposite of love isn't hate, it's indifference. I hate scammers, but I'm pretty indifferent to money.
The best outcome of AI is if it delivers huge amounts of value to society but no profit to anyone.
The Internet delivered huge value but no profit, and that's why it was good.
Let's build technology and open source software that market breaks everything.
Let's demoralize the scammers so hard that they don't even try.
I dream of a day when company valuations halve when I create a GitHub repo.
key_insight: The goal of technology should be to deliver so much value through open source that profit becomes impossible, driving grifters out and returning to the utopian early internet ethos.
